stem: A 32-year-old primigravida at 33 weeks and 5 days gestation presents with worsening frontal headache and epigastric discomfort for 18 hours. She has chronic hypertension managed with labetalol 200 mg twice daily and gestational diabetes controlled with metformin. Foetal movements are present but reduced compared with baseline. She denies visual loss. Her pregnancy has otherwise been uncomplicated. On examination she appears uncomfortable but alert. Reflexes are brisk with 2 beats of ankle clonus. Fundal height is appropriate for dates. CTG shows baseline 145 bpm with normal variability and two accelerations in 20 minutes. Ultrasound 3 days earlier showed estimated foetal weight on the 18th centile with normal umbilical artery Dopplers. Urine protein:creatinine ratio today is 62 mg/mmol. Blood tests show platelets 112 x10^9/L, ALT 86 U/L, AST 79 U/L, creatinine 102 micromol/L, urate 0.42 mmol/L.
lead_in: What is the single best next intervention?

options.text: Increase oral labetalol, repeat blood tests in 12 hours, and continue expectant inpatient monitoring
options.rationale: This is inappropriate because severe-range hypertension with end-organ features requires urgent stabilisation and expedited delivery planning rather than simple escalation of oral therapy. Delay increases maternal seizure and stroke risk. (Source: RANZCOG Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Commence magnesium sulfate, treat severe hypertension acutely, and proceed to delivery after maternal stabilisation
options.rationale: Severe pre-eclampsia with neurologic symptoms and biochemical end-organ involvement after 34 weeks approaches the threshold for maternal-first management. Stabilisation with seizure prophylaxis and blood pressure control should precede delivery. (Source: RANZCOG Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Administer corticosteroids and defer delivery for 48 hours provided CTG remains reassuring
options.rationale: Although corticosteroids may be considered before 34 weeks, maternal severe features override attempts to prolong pregnancy when maternal risk is escalating. Delivery should not be delayed solely for steroid completion. (Source: KEMH Hypertensive Disorders Guideline)

options.text: Arrange urgent caesarean section without seizure prophylaxis because foetal growth restriction is present
options.rationale: Delivery mode depends on obstetric factors and maternal stability, not growth centile alone. Magnesium sulfate remains indicated given severe pre-eclampsia features. (Source: RANZCOG Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Repeat ultrasound Dopplers and continue outpatient review if laboratory values remain stable
options.rationale: Outpatient management is contraindicated with sustained severe hypertension and maternal symptoms suggesting imminent eclampsia risk. This would be unsafe. (Source: KEMH Hypertensive Disorders Guideline)

stem: A 37-year-old primigravid woman at 34 weeks' gestation presents with a 2-day history of worsening frontal headache, flashing lights, and right upper quadrant pain. She has had increasing ankle swelling for a week. Her antenatal record shows normal blood pressure until today, and she is taking aspirin 100 mg daily and iron. She denies vaginal bleeding or contractions. She has no history of renal disease or migraine. On examination she looks uncomfortable and mildly oedematous. Deep tendon reflexes are brisk. The foetal heart rate is normal. The midwife asks whether the patient can just be admitted for rest because the baby is still preterm. The obstetric registrar is concerned about severe pre-eclampsia.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Admit for observation only and repeat blood tests tomorrow
options.rationale: This is not mild gestational hypertension. She has severe-range blood pressure plus symptoms and laboratory features of pre-eclampsia, so observation alone is unsafe. Source: RANZCOG hypertensive disorders guidance.

options.text: Start oral labetalol and plan routine outpatient review
options.rationale: Outpatient management is not appropriate in severe pre-eclampsia. She needs urgent inpatient care with maternal stabilisation and obstetric review. Source: RANZCOG hypertensive disorders guidance.

options.text: Begin magnesium sulfate and urgent delivery planning after maternal stabilisation
options.rationale: She has pre-eclampsia with severe features, including severe hypertension, symptoms, thrombocytopenia, and renal/liver involvement. The next step is maternal stabilisation, including magnesium sulfate, then delivery planning based on maternal and foetal status. Source: RANZCOG hypertensive disorders guidance.

options.text: Administer terbutaline and tocolyse until 37 weeks
options.rationale: Tocolysis is not the management priority in severe pre-eclampsia and may be unsafe. The maternal condition takes precedence over prolonging pregnancy. Source: RANZCOG hypertensive disorders guidance.

options.text: Perform caesarean section immediately without stabilisation
options.rationale: Delivery may ultimately be needed, but maternal stabilisation comes first unless there is a crashing maternal or foetal emergency. Magnesium sulfate and blood pressure control are part of the immediate management. Source: RANZCOG hypertensive disorders guidance.

stem: A 36-year-old multiparous woman develops heavy vaginal bleeding 20 minutes after an uncomplicated vaginal delivery at 39 weeks gestation. Labour was induced for gestational hypertension and lasted 18 hours with oxytocin augmentation. She delivered a 4.3 kg infant and required repair of a second-degree perineal tear. The placenta appeared complete on inspection. She has a history of asthma requiring intermittent salbutamol and experienced postpartum haemorrhage after her previous delivery. On review she feels dizzy and nauseated. The uterus is enlarged and boggy despite ongoing uterine massage. Estimated blood loss is now 1400 mL despite oxytocin infusion and tranexamic acid administration. A registrar asks whether carboprost should be given next because bleeding remains brisk.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next uterotonic intervention?

options.text: Administer intramuscular carboprost because uterine atony persists despite oxytocin infusion
options.rationale: Carboprost is relatively contraindicated in patients with asthma because it may precipitate severe bronchospasm. Alternative uterotonics are preferred. (Source: RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

options.text: Administer intravenous ergometrine while continuing uterine massage and haemorrhage protocol activation
options.rationale: Ergometrine may worsen hypertension and is relatively contraindicated in women with hypertensive disorders. Another uterotonic strategy is preferable. (Source: KEMH Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

options.text: Administer misoprostol while preparing escalation measures for ongoing uterine atony management
options.rationale: This patient has contraindications to both carboprost and ergometrine due to asthma and gestational hypertension. Misoprostol is an appropriate additional uterotonic while escalation continues. (Source: RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

options.text: Proceed directly to hysterectomy because recurrent postpartum haemorrhage predicts failed medical therapy
options.rationale: Surgical escalation may become necessary but further appropriate uterotonic and tamponade measures should usually be attempted first in a haemodynamically manageable patient. (Source: RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

options.text: Withhold further uterotonics and prioritise fibrinogen replacement because coagulopathy is the primary issue
options.rationale: Low fibrinogen requires correction, but uterine atony remains the major ongoing bleeding source and requires simultaneous treatment escalation. (Source: KEMH Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

stem: A 30-year-old primigravida at 41 weeks gestation is in labour following induction for post-dates pregnancy. She received dinoprostone overnight followed by oxytocin augmentation for slow progress. Epidural analgesia was inserted 3 hours ago. She is now fully dilated and has been actively pushing for 70 minutes. The fetus is in occipito-posterior position at +1 station. CTG over the preceding 40 minutes shows a baseline rising from 145 to 170 bpm with minimal variability and recurrent late decelerations after most contractions. There have been no accelerations for 35 minutes. Maternal observations show increasing pyrexia despite intravenous fluids. Vaginal examination confirms caput and moulding. The obstetric registrar considers whether rotational instrumental delivery in theatre would be appropriate because foetal scalp lactate is unavailable due to equipment malfunction. The anaesthetic team advises that transfer to theatre would take approximately 20 minutes.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue pushing for another hour because nulliparous women commonly require prolonged second stage labour
options.rationale: The CTG is pathological with multiple concerning features and ongoing delay risks worsening foetal hypoxia. Expectant management is unsafe. (Source: RANZCOG Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance)

options.text: Perform rotational instrumental delivery in the birth suite because the cervix is fully dilated
options.rationale: Rotational instrumental delivery is inappropriate with high station, significant moulding, and uncertain likelihood of rapid safe birth. Failed instrumental delivery risks further foetal compromise. (Source: RANZCOG Operative Vaginal Birth)

options.text: Stop oxytocin, commence intrauterine resuscitation, and proceed to category 1 caesarean section
options.rationale: This CTG is pathological with foetal tachycardia, minimal variability, and recurrent late decelerations suggesting significant foetal compromise, likely worsened by suspected chorioamnionitis. Immediate intrauterine resuscitation and urgent caesarean delivery are indicated. (Source: RANZCOG Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance)

options.text: Perform foetal scalp stimulation and continue labour if a transient acceleration is observed
options.rationale: Scalp stimulation may help clarify indeterminate tracings, but this tracing is already pathological with multiple persistent abnormalities requiring urgent delivery. (Source: RANZCOG Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance)

options.text: Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics and reassess the CTG after maternal fever resolves
options.rationale: Antibiotics are appropriate for suspected chorioamnionitis but should not delay urgent delivery in the setting of pathological foetal monitoring. (Source: RANZCOG Intrapartum Care)

stem: A 34-year-old G3P2 woman at 35 weeks gestation presents by ambulance with sudden-onset severe abdominal pain and vaginal bleeding that began 45 minutes earlier while at home. She has chronic hypertension treated with nifedipine and continues to smoke 10 cigarettes daily despite counselling. Earlier today she noticed reduced foetal movements. Her previous pregnancies were uncomplicated vaginal deliveries. On arrival she appears distressed and reports constant abdominal pain between contractions. The uterus feels firm and diffusely tender. Vaginal bleeding is moderate in volume and dark red. CTG demonstrates recurrent prolonged decelerations with minimal variability. A bedside ultrasound shows a live singleton fetus in cephalic presentation with an anterior placenta clear of the cervical os. The registrar asks whether additional imaging should be arranged to confirm the diagnosis before proceeding further.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Arrange formal ultrasound with Doppler assessment because placental abruption must be confirmed radiologically
options.rationale: Placental abruption is primarily a clinical diagnosis and ultrasound may be falsely reassuring. Ongoing maternal instability and foetal compromise require urgent intervention. (Source: RANZCOG Antepartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Proceed to urgent caesarean section while initiating maternal resuscitation and blood product support
options.rationale: This patient has suspected placental abruption with maternal instability and pathological CTG indicating foetal compromise. Immediate delivery with simultaneous resuscitation is indicated. (Source: RANZCOG Antepartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Commence tocolysis and corticosteroids because the fetus remains preterm at 35 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Tocolysis is contraindicated in significant placental abruption with maternal bleeding and foetal compromise. Delivery should not be delayed for steroid completion. (Source: KEMH Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

options.text: Perform artificial rupture of membranes and attempt induction because she previously delivered vaginally
options.rationale: The fetus is compromised and the mother is haemodynamically unstable, making induction unsafe and unlikely to achieve timely delivery. (Source: RANZCOG Antepartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Observe with continuous CTG monitoring because vaginal bleeding volume is only moderate
options.rationale: Bleeding severity may underestimate concealed haemorrhage in placental abruption. Maternal shock and pathological CTG mandate urgent delivery. (Source: KEMH Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

stem: A 29-year-old woman, para 2, is 20 minutes after a normal vaginal birth at 39 weeks. The placenta delivered spontaneously, but she now has ongoing heavy vaginal bleeding and has soaked through two pads. She had antenatal iron deficiency and a previous postpartum haemorrhage after her first delivery. The current baby weighed 4.2 kg. She received intrapartum oxytocin and had a prolonged second stage. She has asthma, for which she uses inhaled budesonide-formoterol, and no drug allergies. On examination the uterus feels boggy and enlarged. There are no retained placental fragments visible, and the perineum is intact. The obstetric registrar has already asked the midwife to call for help. Her blood pressure is falling, and she is becoming pale and clammy. The team wants the next most appropriate intervention while uterine massage is underway.
lead_in: What is the single best next intervention?

options.text: Intramuscular ergometrine 250 mcg now
options.rationale: Ergometrine is contraindicated in hypertension and is not the preferred next uterotonic in a woman with asthma. Although the uterus is atonic, a different uterotonic choice is safer here. Source: RANZCOG postpartum haemorrhage guidance.

options.text: Intramuscular carboprost 250 mcg now
options.rationale: Carboprost can worsen bronchospasm and is not ideal in a woman with asthma. Asthma makes this a poor choice despite uterine atony being present. Source: RANZCOG postpartum haemorrhage guidance.

options.text: Intravenous tranexamic acid 1 g now
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid should be given early in postpartum haemorrhage alongside ongoing uterine massage and escalation measures. It is appropriate regardless of whether the final cause is atony, and it is not contraindicated by asthma. Source: RANZCOG postpartum haemorrhage guidance.

options.text: Intramuscular oxytocin 10 IU now
options.rationale: Oxytocin is first-line for uterine atony, but she has already received intrapartum oxytocin and is now actively haemorrhaging. The priority is escalation, including tranexamic acid and further uterotonic strategy. Source: RANZCOG postpartum haemorrhage guidance.

options.text: Immediate hysterectomy without further medical treatment
options.rationale: Surgical escalation may be needed if bleeding is refractory, but this is not the immediate next step while the uterus is being managed medically and the cause appears to be atony. Rapid medical therapy should be started first. Source: RANZCOG postpartum haemorrhage guidance.

stem: A 28-year-old primigravida at 31 weeks and 4 days gestation presents with ongoing clear vaginal fluid loss for 18 hours. She has felt intermittent lower abdominal tightening but no regular contractions. Pregnancy has been uncomplicated apart from iron deficiency anaemia. Speculum examination confirms pooling of liquor in the posterior fornix. Initial CTG is reassuring and ultrasound demonstrates cephalic presentation with reduced amniotic fluid volume. She is admitted for expectant management with corticosteroids and intravenous antibiotics. Thirty-six hours later she develops increasing uterine tenderness and reports feeling feverish. The foetal heart rate baseline rises progressively over the next hour. A resident asks whether delivery should still be deferred because the corticosteroid course will not be complete for another 12 hours.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue expectant management until corticosteroids are completed because CTG variability remains reassuring
options.rationale: Suspected chorioamnionitis is an indication for delivery regardless of steroid completion because ongoing intrauterine infection increases maternal and foetal risk. (Source: RANZCOG Preterm Birth)

options.text: Proceed toward delivery while continuing broad-spectrum antibiotics for presumed chorioamnionitis
options.rationale: Maternal fever, uterine tenderness, inflammatory marker elevation, and foetal tachycardia strongly suggest chorioamnionitis requiring delivery rather than ongoing expectant management. (Source: KEMH PPROM Guideline)

options.text: Commence tocolysis because uterine activity may reduce after antibiotic therapy is escalated
options.rationale: Tocolysis is generally contraindicated in suspected intrauterine infection because it may delay necessary delivery. (Source: KEMH PPROM Guideline)

options.text: Repeat ultrasound and continue observation because foetal tachycardia may reflect maternal dehydration alone
options.rationale: Maternal fever, uterine tenderness, and inflammatory marker elevation make chorioamnionitis substantially more likely than isolated dehydration. (Source: RANZCOG Preterm Birth)

options.text: Stop antibiotics because inflammatory markers commonly rise after corticosteroid administration in pregnancy
options.rationale: Although steroids may mildly influence white cell counts, the overall clinical picture strongly supports infection requiring ongoing treatment and delivery planning. (Source: KEMH PPROM Guideline)

stem: A 27-year-old woman, G1P0, presents with 6 hours of right-sided lower abdominal pain and light vaginal spotting at 7 weeks by last menstrual period. She had a positive urine pregnancy test 10 days ago. Her cycles are usually regular. She is haemodynamically stable but uncomfortable. She has no previous pelvic surgery, no known endometriosis, and no history of infertility treatment. She has had one episode of nausea but no syncope. On examination her abdomen is mildly tender in the right iliac fossa, and there is mild cervical motion tenderness. The cervix is closed. Her partner asks whether the bleeding means she is miscarrying and whether she can just go home if the pain settles. The registrar wants the safest next investigation.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Urgent transvaginal ultrasound
options.rationale: This is the best next investigation in a stable patient with early pregnancy pain and spotting, because transvaginal ultrasound can assess for intrauterine pregnancy or ectopic pregnancy. Her beta-hCG is in the discriminatory zone where ultrasound is useful. Source: RANZCOG early pregnancy guidance.

options.text: Repeat serum beta-hCG in 48 hours only
options.rationale: Serial beta-hCG can help when the initial ultrasound is nondiagnostic, but it is not the single best first investigation here. She is stable and already has an hCG level where transvaginal ultrasound is informative. Source: RANZCOG early pregnancy guidance.

options.text: Dilation and curettage to confirm a failed pregnancy
options.rationale: There is no confirmed intrauterine pregnancy loss to justify uterine evacuation. In early pregnancy pain and bleeding, the priority is localisation of the pregnancy. Source: RANZCOG early pregnancy guidance.

options.text: Abdominal CT scan with contrast
options.rationale: CT is not appropriate first-line imaging in early pregnancy when ultrasound can answer the question without radiation. This would expose the pregnancy unnecessarily. Source: RANZCOG early pregnancy guidance.

options.text: Discharge with reassurance and routine antenatal booking
options.rationale: This presentation is not low risk enough for reassurance alone. Ectopic pregnancy must be excluded before discharge. Source: RANZCOG early pregnancy guidance.

stem: A 23-year-old woman, G2P0, presents to the ED with 2 days of increasing vaginal bleeding at 13 weeks gestation by dates. She has passed small clots and has cramping lower abdominal pain but no syncope. She had an ultrasound 1 week ago that showed an intrauterine pregnancy with a foetal heartbeat. She is blood group O negative, her partner is Rh positive, and she has not yet received anti-D in this pregnancy. She has no fever, no offensive discharge, and no adnexal tenderness. On examination she is haemodynamically stable, with a closed cervix and a uterus appropriate for dates. Her previous pregnancy ended in an early loss at 9 weeks and she is worried this one is happening again. The team asks about the most important immediate prophylaxis while the pregnancy is still ongoing.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer anti-D immunoglobulin now
options.rationale: An Rh-negative woman with first-trimester bleeding and a potentially ongoing pregnancy should receive anti-D prophylaxis according to Australian practice. This reduces the risk of maternal sensitisation. Source: RANZCOG anti-D guidance; KEMH anti-D guideline.

options.text: Provide no prophylaxis because the cervix is closed
options.rationale: A closed cervix does not remove the indication for Rh prophylaxis in an Rh-negative woman with early pregnancy bleeding. Sensitisation risk is related to fetomaternal haemorrhage, not cervical dilation alone. Source: RANZCOG anti-D guidance; KEMH anti-D guideline.

options.text: Arrange urgent suction curettage for inevitable miscarriage
options.rationale: The prior live intrauterine pregnancy and current stable exam do not establish inevitable miscarriage. Management should include prophylaxis and obstetric follow-up rather than immediate evacuation. Source: RANZCOG early pregnancy guidance; RANZCOG anti-D guidance.

options.text: Start methotrexate because ectopic pregnancy remains possible
options.rationale: A recent ultrasound documented an intrauterine pregnancy with cardiac activity, so methotrexate for ectopic pregnancy is inappropriate. The current issue is bleeding in a confirmed intrauterine pregnancy. Source: RANZCOG early pregnancy guidance.

options.text: Repeat beta-hCG in 48 hours and withhold all treatment
options.rationale: Serial beta-hCG is not the key immediate step when an Rh-negative patient has bleeding and already has a documented intrauterine pregnancy. Anti-D prophylaxis should not be delayed. Source: RANZCOG anti-D guidance; KEMH anti-D guideline.

stem: A 39-year-old multiparous woman with gestational diabetes managed on insulin is in the second stage of labour at 40 weeks gestation. Estimated foetal weight at 38 weeks was 4.4 kg. She has been pushing effectively for 35 minutes and the foetal head delivers spontaneously. Immediately afterwards the head retracts tightly against the perineum and fails to restitute. Gentle downward traction does not deliver the shoulders. The room becomes crowded as additional staff arrive. A junior doctor suggests increasing traction because the foetal heart rate has fallen to 70 bpm. The patient is anxious and repeatedly asks whether the baby is stuck. The registrar notes that an episiotomy has not yet been performed and asks which manoeuvre should occur next.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next manoeuvre?

options.text: Apply stronger downward traction because urgent delivery is required to reduce hypoxic injury risk
options.rationale: Excessive traction increases risk of brachial plexus injury and is not recommended in shoulder dystocia management. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Perform McRoberts manoeuvre with suprapubic pressure while avoiding fundal pressure
options.rationale: McRoberts manoeuvre with suprapubic pressure is recommended first-line management for shoulder dystocia and may resolve many cases without further intervention. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Apply fundal pressure to increase expulsive force and facilitate anterior shoulder delivery
options.rationale: Fundal pressure is contraindicated because it worsens impaction and increases maternal and foetal injury risk. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Perform immediate episiotomy before attempting any positional or internal manoeuvres
options.rationale: Episiotomy may facilitate internal manoeuvres later but does not directly relieve bony impaction and is not the first manoeuvre itself. (Source: RCOG Shoulder Dystocia Guideline)

options.text: Proceed directly to posterior arm delivery because foetal bradycardia confirms failed basic manoeuvres
options.rationale: Posterior arm delivery is an important escalation manoeuvre but first-line basic manoeuvres should usually be attempted initially. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia)
